description: Figure 4 presents the mechanical behavior and fracture characteristics of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with varying Zr contents (Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50). Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves demonstrating that increasing Zr addition from 0 to 0.50 wt% simultaneously enhances yield strength (from ~435 MPa to ~505 MPa) and fracture ductility (elongation increasing from ~52% to ~58%), achieving a remarkable strength-ductility synergy. The inset shows the true stress-strain curve of Zr0.50 steel, revealing a true ultimate tensile strength approaching ~1200 MPa. Panels (b)-(d) provide SEM fractographs of the corresponding fracture surfaces: Zr0 exhibits small dimples mixed with cleavage facets, whereas Zr0.01 and Zr0.50 display progressively larger, deeper dimples with increased density, indicating a transition toward more ductile fracture behavior. These results collectively demonstrate that Zr microalloying promotes strain hardening capability and compatible deformation through heterogeneous grain structure, delaying necking and improving fracture ductility.
